controls.name: Symmetric Encryption
controls.description: General implementation of symmetric encryption using a shared secret key for confidentiality. Applicable to in-vehicle communication channels and data-at-rest protection. This is a high-level control that should be refined by specific algorithm implementations.

elapsedTime.rationale: Forces attacker to perform cryptanalysis or side-channel extraction.

equipment.rationale: Side-channel extraction typically requires bespoke physical equipment (oscilloscopes, probes).

controls.name: AES (Advanced Encryption Standard)
controls.description: AES block cipher implementation. Provides high-security confidentiality for automotive data-at-rest and in-transit. Often hardware-accelerated to maintain real-time performance on ECUs.

elapsedTime.rationale: AES is computationally infeasible to brute force within reasonable timeframes.

specialistExpertise.rationale: Requires deep knowledge of block cipher implementation and side-channel analysis.

controls.name: ChaCha20
controls.description: ChaCha20 stream cipher. Offers high performance in software-only environments (no hardware acceleration needed) while maintaining robust resistance against timing attacks.

elapsedTime.rationale: High computational complexity for state recovery.

equipment.rationale: Less susceptible to hardware-based EM/Power side channels if purely software-based, standard IT tools apply.

controls.name: Asymmetric Encryption
controls.description: Asymmetric encryption uses public/private key pairs for secure key exchange, authentication, and digital signatures in automotive systems. It is essential for secure OTA updates, V2X communications, and establishing trust between ECUs and backend servers.

controls.name: RSA (Rivest-Shamir-Adleman)
controls.description: RSA is a foundational asymmetric cryptographic algorithm used for secure data transmission, digital signatures, and secure boot in automotive ECUs.

controls.name: ECC (Elliptic Curve Cryptography)
controls.description: ECC is an efficient asymmetric cryptography method used for secure key exchange and digital signatures in resource-constrained automotive ECUs. ECC provides equivalent security to RSA with smaller key sizes.

controls.name: Network Security
controls.description: Foundational controls designed to protect automotive and cloud-connected systems from network-based threats. Includes measures such as network segmentation, traffic monitoring, and secure communication baselines.

controls.name: TLS (Transport Layer Security)
controls.description: TLS (Transport Layer Security) is a cryptographic protocol for securing data in transit over automotive networks, including backend communications, OTA updates, and telematics. TLS 1.3 provides forward secrecy by default.

controls.name: Rate Limiting
controls.description: Rate limiting restricts the number of requests or messages allowed over a network interface. In vehicles, it prevents DoS attacks on the CAN bus or CPU exhaustion on ECUs.

controls.name: Autoscaling
controls.description: Autoscaling automatically adjusts the number of active resources in cloud environments based on demand, ensuring resilience against traffic spikes and DoS attacks.

controls.name: Network Segmentation
controls.description: Divides automotive networks into isolated segments (e.g., VLANs, physical separate buses), limiting the spread of attacks and restricting lateral movement.

controls.name: Certificate Management and Revocation
controls.description: Ensures digital certificates used for authentication, encryption, and signing in automotive systems are properly issued, renewed, and revoked (e.g., via CRLs or OCSP). Essential for maintaining trust in V2X and OTA.

controls.name: MACsec (Media Access Control Security)
controls.description: MACsec provides encryption and integrity protection at the data link layer for automotive Ethernet networks, securing high-speed in-vehicle communications (e.g., Zonal controllers to Central Compute).

equipment.rationale: Intercepting MACsec requires physical access to the high-speed Ethernet lines and specialized network tapping gear.

controls.name: Secure Key Encapsulation / Exchange
controls.description: Secure key encapsulation and exchange mechanisms provide the foundation for establishing cryptographic session keys over insecure automotive networks, reinforcing network security by protecting key material during transport and session setup.

controls.name: Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange
controls.description: Diffie-Hellman is a protocol for securely exchanging cryptographic keys over insecure automotive networks, enabling secure session establishment. Often used as part of TLS handshakes or V2X.

controls.name: ML-KEM (Module-Lattice-Based Key-Encapsulation Mechanism)
controls.description: ML-KEM (formerly Kyber) is a post-quantum key encapsulation mechanism based on the module learning-with-errors problem. It provides a quantum-resistant method for secure key establishment, protecting automotive communication channels against 'harvest now, decrypt later' quantum attacks.

elapsedTime.rationale: High resistance to cryptanalytic efforts even with quantum acceleration.

specialistExpertise.rationale: Implementation and analysis of lattice-based KEMs require specialized cryptographic expertise.

equipment.rationale: Standard high-end compute capability; no specialized quantum hardware required for defensive implementation.

controls.name: Firewall
controls.description: A firewall monitors and controls incoming and outgoing network traffic based on predetermined rules. In automotive systems, firewalls (often running on the central gateway) segment networks and block unauthorized access.

controls.name: Load Balancer
controls.description: A load balancer distributes incoming network traffic across multiple servers. In connected vehicle ecosystems, load balancers help mitigate denial-of-service attacks on backend infrastructure.

controls.name: Intrusion Detection System (IDS)
controls.description: An IDS monitors network or system activities for malicious actions or policy violations. It does not actively block traffic, but alerts the SOC. Forces an attacker to use slow, evasive techniques to avoid detection.

elapsedTime.rationale: Forces the attacker to use 'low and slow' evasion techniques.

specialistExpertise.rationale: Requires advanced knowledge of IDS evasion (fragmentation, timing).

controls.name: Intrusion Prevention System (IPS)
controls.description: An IPS actively analyzes and takes automated actions to block or prevent detected threats in network traffic, such as dropping malicious packets or quarantining a compromised node.

controls.name: Hashing
controls.description: Hashing is the process of transforming data into a fixed-size string of characters. Used for verifying firmware integrity, authenticating diagnostic messages, protecting secure boot processes, and ensuring data has not been tampered with.

controls.name: SHA-2 Family
controls.description: The SHA-2 family consists of cryptographic hash functions including SHA-224, SHA-256, SHA-384, and SHA-512, which are based on the Merkle-DamgÃ¥rd construction.

controls.name: SHA-3 Family
controls.description: SHA-3 is a modern cryptographic hash function based on the Keccak algorithm. Uses a sponge construction, making it resistant to length extension attacks.

controls.name: MD5
controls.description: MD5 is a legacy hash function sometimes found in older automotive systems. Due to known vulnerabilities (high collision probability), it should be replaced by stronger algorithms.

elapsedTime.rationale: Collisions can be generated in seconds using standard IT equipment.

controls.name: Digital Signature
controls.description: Digital signatures provide a cryptographic means to verify the authenticity and integrity of messages, firmware, or software in automotive systems. Foundational for secure boot, OTA updates, and authenticated diagnostics.

controls.name: DSA (Digital Signature Algorithm)
controls.description: DSA is a standard for generating and verifying digital signatures, used to authenticate firmware, software, and messages in automotive ECUs.

controls.name: ECDSA (Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm)
controls.description: ECDSA is a digital signature algorithm based on elliptic curve cryptography, providing strong security with smaller key sizes. Widely used in automotive V2X, secure diagnostics, and OTA update authentication.

controls.name: EdDSA (Edwards-curve Digital Signature Algorithm)
controls.description: EdDSA is a modern digital signature algorithm designed for high performance and security. It offers inherent resistance to side-channel attacks and nonce-reuse vulnerabilities, making it highly recommended for automotive designs.

controls.name: ML-DSA (Module-Lattice-Based Digital Signature Standard)
controls.description: ML-DSA (formerly Dilithium) is a post-quantum digital signature algorithm based on the hardness of module-lattice problems. It provides quantum-resistant authenticity, integrity, and non-repudiation, making it suitable for future-proof automotive applications like V2X and secure OTA.

elapsedTime.rationale: Breaking lattice-based signatures requires significant computational resources, resistant to quantum speedups.

specialistExpertise.rationale: Requires advanced knowledge of lattice cryptography and quantum-safe protocols.

equipment.rationale: Standard high-performance computing is sufficient, but brute force is infeasible.

controls.name: Message Authentication Codes
controls.description: A Message Authentication Code (MAC) is a short piece of information used to authenticate a message and ensure its integrity and authenticity using a shared secret key.

controls.name: HMAC (Hash-Based Message Authentication Code)
controls.description: HMAC verifies the integrity and authenticity of messages exchanged between ECUs using a cryptographic hash function and a secret key.

controls.name: CMAC (Cipher-based Message Authentication Code)
controls.description: CMAC is a symmetric-key algorithm based on block ciphers like AES, used for message authentication and integrity verification in automotive networks. It is susceptible to block-cipher side-channel attacks if implemented purely in generic hardware.

controls.name: Trusted Execution Environment
controls.description: A Trusted Execution Environment (TEE) is a secure area of a processor that ensures sensitive code and data are isolated from the main operating system. In automotive systems, TEEs protect cryptographic operations, key storage, and security-critical applications from compromise, even if the main OS is attacked.

equipment.rationale: Logical attacks require standard IT equipment; physical glitching requires specialized voltage/clock injection tools.

controls.name: Enclave Runtime Environment
controls.description: The secure software layer or microkernel running within an isolated execution state. It manages secure applications, exposes hardened APIs, and validates boundaries to shield critical logic from the primary OS.

equipment.rationale: Attacking the enclave runtime environment is typically a logical software exploitation exercise via Secure Monitor Calls (SMC).

controls.name: Hardware-Enforced Domain Isolation
controls.description: Silicon-level security mechanisms (such as hardware security extensions, memory encryption, or hardware-based privilege separation) that enforce isolated memory spaces and execution states at the physical layer.

equipment.rationale: Hardware-level bypass of TrustZone typically requires advanced fault injection or SoC decapsulation equipment.

controls.name: Secure RAM
controls.description: Secure RAM is a protected memory region within an ECU or SoC that is isolated from the main system. It is used to store sensitive data such as keys or tokens during runtime to prevent memory dumping and side-channel attacks.

controls.name: Trusted Key Management
controls.description: Trusted Key Management encompasses the secure generation, distribution, storage, rotation, and destruction of cryptographic keys in automotive systems. This is a foundational control refined by specific storage implementations.

controls.name: Encrypted Key Storage
controls.description: Ensures that cryptographic keys are encrypted or cryptographically wrapped when stored on non-volatile storage mediums. This prevents plaintext key extraction via direct logical or physical access to the storage component.

controls.name: Hardware Security Module (HSM)
controls.description: A Hardware Security Module (HSM) is a dedicated on-chip or discrete component designed to securely manage keys and accelerate cryptographic operations. In ECUs, HSMs provide a robust hardware root of trust.

equipment.rationale: Bypassing a modern automotive HSM physically requires advanced fault injection or decapsulation gear.

controls.name: Trusted Platform Module (TPM)
controls.description: A TPM is a specialized discrete chip providing hardware-based security functions, including platform integrity measurement (PCRs) and secure key storage. Often connected via SPI/I2C in automotive gateways or HPCs.

equipment.rationale: Bus sniffing (SPI/I2C) between the SoC and discrete TPM requires basic logic analyzers and PCB access.

controls.name: Secure Element
controls.description: A secure element is a tamper-resistant hardware component dedicated to storing cryptographic keys and executing sensitive operations (e.g., V2X credentials, digital keys). Similar to TPMs but heavily hardened against physical attacks.

specialistExpertise.rationale: Secure elements are specifically designed to thwart physical decapsulation and SCA, requiring elite expertise to crack.

controls.name: One-Time Programmable (OTP) Memory
controls.description: OTP memory is non-volatile storage that can be programmed only once. Used to securely store immutable data such as hardware root keys, device identifiers, or secure boot configuration.

controls.name: eFuses
controls.description: eFuses are hardware-based, programmable fuses used to permanently store security-critical information or permanently disable hardware debug ports (e.g., JTAG) in production.

equipment.rationale: Physically altering a blown eFuse requires focused ion beam (FIB) decapsulation equipment.

controls.name: Access Control
controls.description: Base mechanism restricting access to resources and operations based on policies or system attributes. Protects against unauthorized access and privilege escalation.

controls.name: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)
controls.description: Restricts system access based on assigned roles. Commonly used for managing access to diagnostic tools, firmware updates, and cloud backend resources.

controls.name: Discretionary Access Control (DAC)
controls.description: Allows resource owners to determine access (e.g., Linux file permissions). Prone to misconfiguration and is often bypassed by basic local privilege escalation.

controls.name: Mandatory Access Control (MAC)
controls.description: Enforces strict, system-determined access policies (e.g., SELinux, AppArmor). Prevents compromised apps from breaking out of their containment even if running as root.

specialistExpertise.rationale: Bypassing MAC usually requires discovering a zero-day kernel exploit.

controls.name: Whitelisting
controls.description: Allows only explicitly approved entities (apps, IPs, CAN IDs) to execute or transmit. Prevents unauthorized code execution.

controls.name: Blacklisting
controls.description: Blocks known malicious entities. Weak control easily bypassed by altering signatures or addresses.

controls.name: Secure Boot
controls.description: Ensures that only firmware and software images with valid digital signatures from trusted authorities are allowed to execute during system startup, establishing a chain of trust.

specialistExpertise.rationale: Requires either stealing the OEM signing key or using advanced voltage/clock glitching to skip the verification instruction.

controls.name: Filesystem Integrity
controls.description: Ensures files and directories on ECUs have not been tampered with. Critical for detecting rootkits and protecting static configuration data.

controls.name: DM-verity (Device Mapper Verity)
controls.description: Kernel feature that provides transparent integrity checking of block devices using Merkle trees. Ensures read-only root filesystems cannot be tampered with offline.

controls.name: DM-integrity (Device Mapper Integrity)
controls.description: Provides transparent integrity protection for block devices by storing and verifying checksums for each data block. Defends against offline tampering.

controls.name: Filesystem Encryption
controls.description: Protects confidentiality of files/directories by encrypting data at rest. Ensures sensitive PII or credentials cannot be read if physical storage is stolen.

equipment.rationale: Bypass requires physical extraction of RAM (cold boot) or advanced SoC decapsulation to find the key.

controls.name: DM-crypt
controls.description: Transparent disk encryption subsystem in Linux. Often used with LUKS to manage the master key dynamically.

controls.name: Authenticated Diagnostics
controls.description: Requires cryptographic authentication (e.g., UDS 0x29) before allowing critical diagnostic functions, preventing unauthorized reprogramming or data extraction.

controls.name: Secure Debug Interface
controls.description: Restricts or physically disables hardware debug ports (JTAG/SWD) in production vehicles. Defends against firmware dumping and memory manipulation.

specialistExpertise.rationale: Re-enabling disabled JTAG pins often requires board-level micro-soldering or SoC decapsulation.

controls.name: Logging and Audit
controls.description: Records security-relevant events in a tamper-evident manner. Acts as a detective control and supports non-repudiation.

controls.name: Secure Software Lifecycle Management
controls.description: Ensures security is integrated into SDLC (threat modeling, secure code reviews). Reduces the probability of introducing vulnerabilities into production code.

controls.name: Software Testing
controls.description: Systematic process (fuzzing, SAST/DAST, pen testing) to identify defects before deployment.

controls.name: Vulnerability Management
controls.description: Ongoing process of patching and remediating known security vulnerabilities. Protects against N-day exploits.

controls.name: Secure Firmware Update
controls.description: Mechanisms (OTA) ensuring only authenticated, integrity-checked firmware is installed. Defends against malicious flashing.

controls.name: Anti-Rollback Protection
controls.description: Prevents installation of older, vulnerable firmware versions. Utilizes hardware monotonic counters or eFuses.

equipment.rationale: Bypassing hardware-enforced rollback usually requires decapsulating the chip and altering the eFuses or NVRAM directly.

controls.name: Cryptographic Randomness Service
controls.description: Provides unpredictable, uniformly distributed random numbers necessary for generating cryptographic keys, nonces, initialization vectors (IVs), and salts across the platform.

controls.name: Cryptographically Secure Pseudo-Random Number Generator (CSPRNG)
controls.description: A CSPRNG generates random numbers that are suitable for cryptographic use, ensuring unpredictability for keys, nonces, and other security parameters.

controls.name: True Random Number Generator (TRNG)
controls.description: A hardware-based entropy source that leverages physical, non-deterministic phenomena (such as thermal noise, clock jitter, or quantum effects) to produce raw, high-entropy bits.

controls.name: Resource Management
controls.description: Allocates and controls system resources (CPU, Memory, Network) to ensure stability and prevent exhaustion.

controls.name: Scheduling
controls.description: Manages task execution to guarantee real-time performance and prevent CPU hogging by malicious or faulty processes.

controls.name: Resource Limiting
controls.description: Enforces max usage quotas (e.g., Linux Cgroups) to prevent single processes from taking down an ECU.

controls.name: Replay Protection
controls.description: Prevents attackers from retransmitting previously captured valid messages or commands. Essential for remote vehicle access (KeyFob), diagnostics, and Secure Onboard Communication (SecOC).

controls.name: Monotonic Counters
controls.description: Monotonic counters are non-decreasing numeric values used to track the order of events. Foundational for replay protection (SecOC) and anti-rollback protection for firmware.

controls.name: System Monitoring and Health Checks
controls.description: System monitoring and health checks involve continuously observing and evaluating the operational status of a system to detect anomalies or failures. This control is essential for maintaining system reliability and identifying potential security issues before they can be exploited.

controls.name: Watchdog Timer
controls.description: A Watchdog Timer (WDT) is a hardware or software mechanism that automatically resets the microcontroller or SoC if the system hangs or enters an unintended state. Crucial for mitigating denial-of-service states and recovering from transient fault injection attacks.
